title: The 9am digest that vanished
tagline: Every morning at 9:00 the push queue hits its cap for 15 seconds and silently drops 10,000 notifications.
brief: You run a push-notification pipeline: an ingest API enqueues jobs onto a bounded broker, a worker fleet delivers them, receipts land in Postgres. Someone capped the queue at maxDepth 2,000 'to keep broker memory tight'. Steady state is healthy — 300 jobs/s in, 400/s of drain. Then the 9am daily digest fires: 1,200 jobs/s for 15 seconds. Unlike the queue-backlog drill (a SUSTAINED producer/consumer rate mismatch that grows forever), this system is fine on average — it only dies during the burst, because the buffer is smaller than the burst's excess.
symptom: Watch the queue's card when the burst phase starts at t=20s: depth rockets from 0 to the 2,000 cap in ~3 seconds, memory% pins at 100, and the log fills with 'Queue overflow ... DROPPED' — error rate 67% at the peak. Of the 18,000 burst messages, only ~8,000 survive; the broker is also one bad tick from an OOM crash while pinned at the cap. By t=55 everything looks healthy again, which is exactly why nobody caught it for a week.

schemaMemory.aiNotes: Write-heavy receipt store; one row per delivered push.

diagnosis.question: During the 15-second digest burst the queue's error rate spikes to 67% and the broker logs 'Queue overflow ... DROPPED'. Before and after the burst, every graph is flat and green. What's the root cause?

options.text: The bounded buffer is smaller than the burst's excess: (1,200 − 400) × 15s = 12,000 messages need buffering, but maxDepth is 2,000 — the other 10,000 overflow and drop.
options.explain: Right. A bounded queue must absorb the burst INTEGRAL, not the burst rate: excess = (burst rps − drain rps) × burst seconds. Here that's 12,000 against a 2,000-slot buffer, so the cap is hit ~3s into the burst and everything above it is dropped for the remaining 12 seconds. Steady state being healthy is exactly the signature — the system is sized for the average, not the burst.

options.text: Consumers are too slow for the workload — a sustained producer/consumer rate mismatch, the classic ever-growing backlog.
options.explain: A sustained mismatch shows depth growing linearly forever with no plateau — that's the queue-backlog scenario. Here drain (400/s) exceeds baseline arrivals (300/s), so depth sits at zero for the first 20 seconds and fully recovers by t=55. The failure window is exactly the 15-second burst, which means it's a buffer-sizing problem, not a steady-state throughput problem.

options.text: Postgres saturates during the burst and backpressures the delivery workers, stalling consumption until the queue overflows.
options.explain: Check the downstream cards during the burst: the workers and Postgres never receive more than the queue's drain rate of 400/s — the broker meters the flow regardless of how fast producers enqueue. Postgres sits around 5% utilization with zero errors the whole run, and the 'overflow ... DROPPED' log is emitted by the broker at enqueue time, upstream of the workers entirely.

options.text: The Ingest API tier saturates at 1,200 rps and its retries amplify the burst into the queue.
options.explain: Ten instances at 5 ms/request handle 2,000 rps, so 1,200 rps is only 60% utilization — the ingest card shows zero errors throughout the burst. There is also no retryPolicy configured on any wire, so nothing can amplify. The queue really is receiving exactly 1,200/s; the problem is what it does with the 800/s it can't drain.

fix.objective: Survive the 9am burst without dropping a single job: absorb the 15-second spike in the buffer AND drain the backlog within seconds of the burst ending.

criteria.label: The queue drops nothing — even mid-burst
Backlog is drained within 5s of the burst ending
Nothing crashes — the broker stays up

hints: Size a bounded queue for the burst integral: excess = (burst rps − drain rps) × burst seconds. Right now that's (1,200 − 400) × 15 = 12,000 messages against a 2,000-slot buffer.

Click the Push Queue node — you have two knobs. maxDepth alone absorbs the burst but leaves a 12,000-deep backlog draining at only 100/s net, failing the drain criterion. More consumersPerGroup alone lifts drain to 1,000/s but the peak backlog (200/s × 15s = 3,000) still overflows the 2,000 cap.

Combine them: consumersPerGroup 10 makes drain 1,000/s, capping the peak backlog at (1,200 − 1,000) × 15 = 3,000 — and maxDepth 20,000 holds that at 15% memory. After the burst, 1,000/s drain against 300/s arrivals clears the backlog in ~4 seconds.
